related_questions.question: Do you pay tax on inheritance in BC?
related_questions.snippet: British Columbia does not impose an inheritance tax, and across Canada, there is no inheritance tax. The BC government followed the federal government, getting rid of gift taxes and succession duties in BC in 1977.
related_questions.title: Inheritance Tax BC: What You Need to Know in 2025

related_questions.question: What is the cheapest way to do a will in BC?
related_questions.snippet: What Is the Cheapest Way to Do a Will in BC? The most affordable option is often to prepare your will using an online platform or documents online to create legal documents, but it must still meet all legal requirements to be legally valid under provincial laws.
related_questions.title: How Much Does It Cost to Make a Will in BC? - Onyx Law Group

related_questions.question: What not to do when separating?
related_questions.snippet: Neglecting Financial Protection Leaving joint accounts, credit cards, or mortgages open after separation can lead to disputes or financial difficulties. Your ex-partner could continue using these accounts, leaving you liable for debts.
related_questions.title: 10 Common Mistakes People Make During Separation and How to ...

related_questions.question: What is the average spousal support payment in BC?
related_questions.snippet: The spousal support amount ranges from 1.5 to 2 percent of the difference between the spouses' gross incomes for each year of the relationship up to a maximum of 50 percent. For marriages of 25 years or longer, the range is from 37.5 to 50 percent of the income difference, practically equalizing the incomes.
related_questions.title: How is Spousal Support / Alimony Calculated in British Columbia?

related_questions.question: How do you legally separate in BC?
related_questions.snippet: There is no such thing as a “legal” separation. You do not need to apply for anything in court to be separated. A married couple or a couple living in a married like relationship are separated when at least one of them decides to separate and stop living together.
related_questions.title: Separation and Divorce Introduction | Supreme Court BC

related_questions.question: Can you do a legal separation without a lawyer?
related_questions.snippet: Although parties can draw up a separation agreement without the assistance of lawyers, it is often risky to do so. Without knowledge of their legal rights, the parties may draft an agreement that can create problems in the future or fail to address all of the issues between them.
related_questions.title: Separation Agreements - People's Law Library

related_questions.question: What are the 3 C's of divorce?

text_blocks.snippet: The "3 C's of Divorce" refer to Communication, Cooperation, and Compromise, principles that help make the separation process smoother, especially when children are involved, by reducing conflict and focusing on collaborative solutions for finances, assets, and co-parenting. Some variations also mention Custody or Control as key factors, but the core idea is a respectful, functional approach to ending a marriage.

list.snippet: Communication: Maintaining open, respectful dialogue is crucial for resolving issues, understanding expectations, and co-parenting effectively.
Cooperation: Working together, rather than against each other, to handle practical matters like finances and child-rearing fosters a less adversarial environment.
Compromise: Both parties must be willing to give and take on issues like asset division and parenting time to reach agreements efficiently.
For Children: Focusing on these C's protects children from the harmful effects of high-conflict divorce, promoting resilience and stability.
For the Process: Following these principles can lead to quicker, less expensive, and less emotionally draining divorces, as courts often favor parties who demonstrate good behavior.
Control: Taking responsibility for your own reactions and choices rather than letting emotions dictate the process.
Custody: A major area where communication, cooperation, and compromise are essential for child arrangements.

related_questions.question: What money can't be touched in a divorce?
related_questions.snippet: The most common examples are gifted and inherited assets. Money or property given to one spouse as a gift, or received through an inheritance, is generally considered separate property and cannot be touched in a divorce, as long as it has been kept separate.
related_questions.title: What Money Cannot Be Touched in a Divorce?

related_questions.question: What is the most you can inherit without paying taxes?
related_questions.snippet: The 2026 federal exemption is $15M per person — but some states tax inheritances. Estate taxes, inheritance taxes, capital gains taxes, and income taxes can potentially apply. Step-up rules may reduce taxes on inherited assets like stocks and real estate.
related_questions.title: Taxes on inheritance & how to avoid them - Empower

related_questions.question: How to avoid probate tax in BC?
related_questions.snippet: One way to avoid probate is to have all assets in the names or more than one person as Joint Tenants. Joint tenancy allows for the passing of the asset to the surviving party with little cost, and certainly no probate fees.
related_questions.title: Probate Avoidance - B.C. Real Estate Lawyers

related_questions.question: What is the first thing you should do when you inherit money?
related_questions.snippet: The first significant step after receiving your inheritance should be finding professionals to help you manage it. Solidify your short-and long-term financial goals to develop a solid, sustainable plan. Don't make any large or high-risk investments before consulting with a trusted advisor.
related_questions.title: What to Do with an Inheritance: Making the Right Choices
